[Case Introduction]  
Chief Complaint: Sudden right-sided limb weakness with altered consciousness for 4 hours  
Present Illness History: The patient was found by family members 4 hours ago with right-sided limb weakness, difficulty in lifting, inability to stand or walk, accompanied by altered consciousness, lethargy, slow response, slurred speech, and nausea and vomiting. Movement was observed in the left limbs, with no limb twitching, and was subsequently sent to the emergency department for evaluation.  
Past Medical History: History of "circumcision" 4 years ago; recurrent chest pain episodes in the past 2 years, located in the precordial area, without chest tightness or shortness of breath, and no respiratory distress, untreated. Denies history of hypertension or diabetes.  
Personal History: Student, no smoking, drinking, or other harmful habits.  
Physical Examination: T: 36.3°C, P: 73 beats/min, R: 18 breaths/min, BP: 116/71 mmHg  
Lethargic state, soft spirit, speech not fluent, pupils equal and round, 3 mm in diameter, sluggish light reflex, both eyes gaze to the left, no spontaneous nystagmus, symmetrical forehead wrinkles, shallow right nasolabial fold, uncooperative tongue extension, clear breath sounds in both lungs, no dry or wet rales, regular heart rhythm, no murmurs, abdomen soft, liver and spleen not enlarged. Muscle strength and sensation uncooperative, no muscle contraction in the right limbs with pain stimulation, left side can be lifted off the bed, decreased muscle tone in the right limbs, normal muscle tone in the left limbs, tendon reflexes (++), positive Babinski sign on the right, negative Babinski sign on the left. NIHSS score 31, GCS score 9, mRS score 5.  
Auxiliary Examinations: Emergency blood gas analysis: pH 7.41, pCO2 41.9 mmHg, pO2 164.0 mmHg, lactate 1.1 mmol/L; hs-CRP < 0.50 mg/L; complete blood count: white blood cell count 16.17 x 10^9/L, neutrophil percentage 0.893, lymphocyte percentage 0.069, red blood cell count 5.29 x 10^12/L, hemoglobin 155 g/L, hematocrit 0.442, platelet count 362 x 10^9/L; emergency biochemistry, myocardial enzyme profile: potassium 3.48 mmol/L, sodium 136.7 mmol/L, chloride 97.4 mmol/L; coagulation profile normal. Emergency cranial CT: decreased density in the left frontal and temporal lobes, likely cerebral infarction. CTA of the brain and neck: left middle cerebral artery M1 segment unclear, considering severe stenosis or occlusion, left internal carotid artery siphon region lumen relatively narrow. Echocardiogram: tricuspid regurgitation (mild), EF: 73%. Routine ECG: sinus rhythm, normal ECG. In the past month:  
The patient has no heart failure;  
The patient has no septicemia;  
The patient has no pneumonia;  
The patient has not undergone plaster fixation, no hip, pelvic, or leg fractures;  
The patient has no multiple trauma;  
The patient has no acute spinal cord injury leading to paralysis.  

The patient has no varicose veins, leg swelling, no central venous access, no history of DVT/PE, no family history of thrombosis, no positive factor V Leiden, prothrombin 20210A negative, no elevated serum homocysteine, no elevated antiphospholipid antibodies, lupus anticoagulant negative, no heparin-induced thrombocytopenia, no other congenital or acquired thrombophilia.  

The patient has no history of inflammatory bowel disease, normal body mass index, no acute myocardial infarction, no chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, no malignant tumors, no other risk factors.

Basic Information: Male, 74 years old, farmer  
Treatment Outcome: Condition improved  
Key Points of the Case: Chronic renal failure, compensated stage  

[Case Introduction]  
Chief Complaint:  
Bilateral lower limb edema for more than twenty days  

Present Illness History:  
The patient developed bilateral lower limb edema without obvious inducement for more than twenty days, presenting as symmetrical pitting edema, accompanied by fatigue, occasional mild edema of the eyelids in the morning, which alleviates in the afternoon, occasional palpitations and shortness of breath, no chills or fever, no dizziness or headache, no cough or sputum, no chest pain or tightness, no nausea or vomiting, no abdominal distension, abdominal pain, or diarrhea, no urinary incontinence or limb movement disorders. The patient did not receive treatment at home. This morning, the patient experienced dizziness, which improved after taking antihypertensive medication, accompanied by discomfort and swelling in the posterior neck and shoulder. The patient came to our hospital today and was admitted to our department with a diagnosis of "chronic renal insufficiency." Since the onset, the patient has had poor spirit, appetite, and sleep, normal urination and defecation, slightly decreased physical strength, and no significant change in weight.

The patient has a history of hypertension for many years, has been taking medication at home, and the blood pressure control status is unclear; has a history of elevated blood sugar; has a history of glaucoma, previously underwent surgical treatment at our hospital; has a history of gout for many years, with a history of right shoulder joint pain for many years; denies a history of heart disease, no history of hepatitis, tuberculosis, or other infectious diseases, no history of trauma or drug allergies.

    "name": "gender", "claim_value": "Male",
    "evidence_value": "Male", "correct": correct_gender
})

# ── STEP 2: calculate using evidence values ─────────────────────────────────
# Formula: GFR = 142 x (Scr/A)^B x 0.9938^age x gender_coefficient
calculator_type = "equation-based"
formula         = "GFR (mL/min/1.73 m²) = 142 x (Scr/A)^B x 0.9938^age x gender_coefficient"
substitution    = f"142 x ({evidence_scr}/{0.9})^(-1.2) x 0.9938^{evidence_age} x 1"

[Case Introduction]  
Chief Complaint:  
[Discussion] 3.1 Overview of the Disease and Causes: Currently, there is no literature reporting the incidence of Castleman Disease (CD). Reports indicate that the average age of patients abroad is 35.6 years, while in China it is 33.1 years. The incidence is more common in females, with a male-to-female ratio of 1:4, although some literature reports no gender difference in incidence. Clinically, CD can be divided into localized (Unicentric CD, UCD) and multicentric (Multicentric CD, MCD). UCD is more common in youth, with a median age of about 20 years, where 90% of localized cases are of the hyaline vascular type and 10% are of the plasma cell type. MCD is less common than UCD, with a median age of 57 years and a male-to-female incidence ratio of 1:2. The disease can occur in any area where lymph nodes are present, with reports indicating that the mediastinum is the most common site in China (60%-70%), followed by the neck (10%-14%), abdomen (5%-10%), and axilla (2%-4%). International reports indicate that the most common sites for localized cases are the mediastinum (30%), neck (23%), abdomen (20%), retroperitoneal space (17%), axilla (5%), groin (3%), and pelvis (2%). Previous studies on the etiology of CD suggest that infection with the gamma herpesvirus HHV-8, elevated serum interleukin-6 levels, VEGF-guided angiogenesis, and atypical proliferation of follicular dendritic cells are correlated with the occurrence and development of CD. 3.2 Clinical Manifestations: UCD patients often have no subjective symptoms and are incidentally found to have painless masses during imaging examinations. A small number of patients may experience cough, dyspnea, or other systemic symptoms. Most UCD patients present with non-progressive clinical manifestations. In contrast, MCD is associated with systemic manifestations of inflammation: fever, night sweats, weight loss, and fatigue. MCD patients often have peripheral lymphadenopathy affecting multiple lymph nodes in the same region, with abnormal laboratory test results such as anemia, hypoalbuminemia, hypergammaglobulinemia, and elevated ESR, IL-6, and IL-10 levels. When these clinical manifestations and laboratory abnormalities occur simultaneously, they often suggest the possibility of MCD. Imaging examinations typically show space-occupying lesions or lymphadenopathy without obvious characteristics. Castleman disease occurring in mediastinal lymph nodes often appears as high-density mass shadows on chest X-rays, with well-defined or lobulated margins, which can be solitary or multiple, commonly showing indirect signs of widened mediastinum, affecting lung parenchyma. Ultrasound examinations typically show homogeneous hypoechoic masses with clear and intact capsules; larger lesions (greater than 5 cm) may appear as heterogeneous hypoechoic masses with uneven capsules. CT examinations typically show soft tissue mass shadows, with dynamic scanning changes resembling arteries. MRI examinations typically show low signal on T1-weighted images and high signal on T2-weighted images. MCD may also show splenomegaly and abdominal or pleural effusion on imaging examinations. This case presents as a space-occupying lesion in the right neck region II, with relatively consistent internal structure, clear boundaries, no obvious adhesion to surrounding tissues, and imaging characteristics of a benign tumor without specific changes. 3.3 Diagnosis and Differential Diagnosis: Due to the different sites of disease occurrence, diverse clinical manifestations, and non-specific clinical symptoms, preoperative diagnosis is challenging. Preoperative CT examinations are somewhat helpful in establishing the diagnosis, showing lesions as homogeneous density, with smooth or irregular soft tissue mass margins. CT enhancement scans show that the enhancement degree of hyaline vascular type UCD lesions is slightly lower than or synchronously significantly enhanced with adjacent large blood vessels; the significant enhancement of the mass is due to the presence of numerous blood vessels and rich capillaries, while plasma cell UCD shows uneven mild to moderate enhancement due to fewer vascular components, lacking characteristic manifestations. MCD typically shows no obvious mass on CT, often presenting one or more lymph nodes of similar size, with mild to moderate enhancement. In the latter two cases, the imaging findings are atypical, making preoperative diagnosis difficult. Early definitive diagnosis of this disease ultimately relies on histopathological examination. For CD, regardless of clinical or pathological classification, there are common histological features: ① intact basic structure of lymph nodes; ② lymphoid follicles and vascular proliferation, with glassy degeneration of vessels in hyaline vascular type follicles and atrophy of germinal centers; plasma cell type shows numerous plasma cells in the follicular stroma and hyperplasia of germinal centers. Frizzera proposed diagnostic criteria for CD: ① Diagnostic criteria for UCD: single-site lymphadenopathy, histopathologically characteristic proliferation, exclusion of possible other primary diseases, generally no systemic symptoms or anemia, elevated immunoglobulins, etc. (except for plasma cell type), and long-term survival after tumor resection; ② Diagnostic criteria for MCD: characteristic histopathological changes, significant lymphadenopathy affecting multiple peripheral lymph nodes, multi-system involvement, and exclusion of other causes. In differential diagnosis, it should be distinguished from malignant lymphoma, lymph node tuberculosis, and nonspecific lymphadenitis. The differentiation from malignant lymphoma includes the following points: in malignant lymphoma, the lymph node structure is almost completely destroyed, with few vascular proliferations, and there is proliferation of monoclonal lymphocytes or Reed-Sternberg cells. For common diseases presenting with lymphadenopathy, such as nonspecific lymphadenitis and lymph node tuberculosis, the key points of differentiation are: ① on physical examination, the former often has tenderness upon palpation, while the latter has no obvious tenderness; ② blood biochemical tests may show elevated neutrophil levels in the former; ③ PPD test differentiation. 3.4 Treatment and Prognosis: Based on previous studies, CD should be treated surgically by excising the abnormal lymph nodes. For UCD, excision of the affected lymph nodes is the most effective method. Clinical follow-up by foreign scholars indicates that UCD has little tendency to recur after surgical treatment. It is worth mentioning that patients with systemic symptoms often have symptom resolution after excision of the affected lymph nodes. However, there are also individual reports indicating that UCD recurred 9 years after surgical treatment. A recent meta-analysis based on 278 case reports found that the mortality rate for patients undergoing surgical treatment for UCD is 3.8%, while for other treatment methods it is 17.6%. Therefore, although surgical methods are very effective, there is also a risk of failure and recurrence. Lesions that cannot be completely excised will progress, and more of these cases will either remain stable or show no obvious symptoms. Rituximab is a drug used to treat MCD and has also been attempted in patients with UCD that cannot be cleared, achieving good results. As for MCD, due to limitations in examination methods, surgical excision is not the primary treatment method. Immunotherapy and chemotherapy are the basic treatment strategies for MCD. Prognostic assessment for MCD is generally more conservative than for UCD. Currently, there are no complete randomized trial studies on MCD treatment methods, and the choice of treatment is limited by existing case reports. Rituximab used alone or in combination with cell inhibitors has shown effectiveness in many patients. Clinical responses to rituximab include a decrease in serum IL-6 levels and a decrease in cytokines in other HIV-positive patient cases as HHV-8 virus levels decline. In more severe disease cases, when signs of organ failure and decreased physical strength appear, rituximab combined with chemotherapy drugs such as etoposide can increase the 5-year survival rate to 90%. However, chemotherapy drugs must be used in combination regimens: monoclonal antibody therapy combined with vincristine or etoposide can suppress MCD, but once discontinued, symptoms will recur within weeks. The latest chemotherapy regimens, such as CHOP (cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone) or CVAD (cyclophosphamide, pirarubicin, vincristine, and dexamethasone), have shown durable efficacy in over 50% of patients. 3.5 Related Disease Course: Although CD is primarily a disease characterized by lymph node lesions, both UCD and MCD may be associated with the occurrence of other diseases. In a series of cases in France, 8 UCD patients were found to have concurrent malignant lymphoma, of which 6 were B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma and 2 were Hodgkin lymphoma. Among the 8 patients, 3 had both UCD and NHL, even though the average diagnostic interval between the former and the latter was 46 months, and the pathophysiological relationship between the two remains unclear. Additionally, nearly 15% of UCD patients develop paraneoplastic pemphigus. MCD patients have similar associated diseases and a higher probability of multi-organ dysfunction compared to UCD patients. For example, MCD patients have a higher risk of malignant tumors, such as Kaposi's sarcoma and hematologic malignancies. 13% of MCD patients also have Kaposi's sarcoma. NHL has a significant relationship with MCD: 15% to 20% of patients have concurrent or developed NHL, with a poor prognosis and a mortality rate as high as 85%. HHV and HIV co-infection is considered an important risk factor for the development of NHL from MCD. Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma is the most common lymphoma in HHV-positive MCD patients, which is also a unique classification in the 2008 WHO classification of malignant tumors of lymphoid origin. Although the underlying mechanisms have yet to be determined, Hodgkin's disease may be related to MCD. MCD is also associated with POEMS syndrome, which includes polyneuropathy (P), organomegaly (O), endocrinopathy (E), monoclonal gammopathy (M, also known as M-protein), and skin changes (S).  
Source: Renaguz Abudumijiti, Li Shihao, Huang Zixian, et al. Clinical report and literature review of cervical Castleman disease. Journal of Oral Disease Prevention, 2017, 25(2):110-114.

Basic Information: Male, 46 years old, farmer

[Case Introduction]
Chief Complaint:
Thirst, polyuria, and polydipsia for over 101 days, with elevated blood sugar discovered 2 days ago.
Present Illness:
The patient was hospitalized for "IgA nephropathy" over 10 days ago at another hospital, receiving "injectable methylprednisolone sodium succinate" 0.5g in intravenous drip for 3 days. On the second day of steroid pulse therapy, the patient experienced thirst, polyuria, and polydipsia, with a water intake of about 5000-6000ml/day and urine output of about 5500ml/day. Blood sugar was not measured at that time, and no special treatment was given. After steroid pulse therapy, the patient was switched to "methylprednisolone tablets" 48mg qd combined with "thalidomide" 50mg qd orally, with instructions to recheck after 1 week of oral steroids. After discharge, the patient took medication on time but still experienced thirst, polyuria, and polydipsia, with symptoms unchanged. Additionally, there were episodes of dizziness, fatigue, blurred vision, and occasional abdominal discomfort. No headache, nausea, or vomiting; no palpitations, shortness of breath, chest tightness, or wheezing; no abdominal pain or diarrhea; no limb numbness or muscle weakness; no weight loss. Two days ago, the patient visited the Eastern Theater General Hospital in Nanjing for re-examination, where fasting blood sugar was 8.89mmol/L, and random blood sugar measured 28.5mmol/L one day prior. [Case Introduction]  
Chief Complaint:  
[Discussion] 3.1 Overview of the Disease and Causes: Currently, there is no literature reporting the incidence of Castleman Disease (CD). Reports indicate that the average age of patients abroad is 35.6 years, while in China it is 33.1 years. The incidence is more common in females, with a male-to-female ratio of 1:4, although some literature reports no gender difference in incidence. Clinically, CD can be divided into localized (Unicentric CD, UCD) and multicentric (Multicentric CD, MCD). UCD is more common in youth, with a median age of about 20 years, where 90% of localized cases are of the hyaline vascular type and 10% are of the plasma cell type. MCD is less common than UCD, with a median age of 57 years and a male-to-female incidence ratio of 1:2. The disease can occur in any area where lymph nodes are present, with reports indicating that the mediastinum is the most common site in China (60%-70%), followed by the neck (10%-14%), abdomen (5%-10%), and axilla (2%-4%). International reports indicate that the most common sites for localized cases are the mediastinum (30%), neck (23%), abdomen (20%), retroperitoneal space (17%), axilla (5%), groin (3%), and pelvis (2%). Previous studies on the etiology of CD suggest that infection with the gamma herpesvirus HHV-8, elevated serum interleukin-6 levels, VEGF-guided angiogenesis, and atypical proliferation of follicular dendritic cells are correlated with the occurrence and development of CD. 3.2 Clinical Manifestations: UCD patients often have no subjective symptoms and are incidentally found to have painless masses during imaging examinations. A small number of patients may experience cough, dyspnea, or other systemic symptoms. Most UCD patients present with non-progressive clinical manifestations. In contrast, MCD is associated with systemic manifestations of inflammation: fever, night sweats, weight loss, and fatigue. MCD patients often have peripheral lymphadenopathy affecting multiple lymph nodes in the same region, with abnormal laboratory test results such as anemia, hypoalbuminemia, hypergammaglobulinemia, and elevated ESR, IL-6, and IL-10 levels. When these clinical manifestations and laboratory abnormalities occur simultaneously, they often suggest the possibility of MCD. Imaging examinations typically show space-occupying lesions or lymphadenopathy without obvious characteristics. Castleman disease occurring in mediastinal lymph nodes often appears as high-density mass shadows on chest X-rays, with well-defined or lobulated margins, which can be solitary or multiple, commonly showing indirect signs of widened mediastinum, affecting lung parenchyma. Ultrasound examinations typically show homogeneous hypoechoic masses with clear and intact capsules; larger lesions (greater than 5 cm) may appear as heterogeneous hypoechoic masses with uneven capsules. CT examinations typically show soft tissue mass shadows, with dynamic scanning changes resembling arteries. MRI examinations typically show low signal on T1-weighted images and high signal on T2-weighted images. MCD may also show splenomegaly and abdominal or pleural effusion on imaging examinations. This case presents as a space-occupying lesion in the right neck region II, with relatively consistent internal structure, clear boundaries, no obvious adhesion to surrounding tissues, and imaging characteristics of a benign tumor without specific changes. 3.3 Diagnosis and Differential Diagnosis: Due to the different sites of disease occurrence, diverse clinical manifestations, and non-specific clinical symptoms, preoperative diagnosis is challenging. Preoperative CT examinations are somewhat helpful in establishing the diagnosis, showing lesions as homogeneous density, with smooth or irregular soft tissue mass margins. CT enhancement scans show that the enhancement degree of hyaline vascular type UCD lesions is slightly lower than or synchronously significantly enhanced with adjacent large blood vessels; the significant enhancement of the mass is due to the presence of numerous blood vessels and rich capillaries, while plasma cell UCD shows uneven mild to moderate enhancement due to fewer vascular components, lacking characteristic manifestations. MCD typically shows no obvious mass on CT, often presenting one or more lymph nodes of similar size, with mild to moderate enhancement. In the latter two cases, the imaging findings are atypical, making preoperative diagnosis difficult. Early definitive diagnosis of this disease ultimately relies on histopathological examination. For CD, regardless of clinical or pathological classification, there are common histological features: ① intact basic structure of lymph nodes; ② lymphoid follicles and vascular proliferation, with glassy degeneration of vessels in hyaline vascular type follicles and atrophy of germinal centers; plasma cell type shows numerous plasma cells in the follicular stroma and hyperplasia of germinal centers. Frizzera proposed diagnostic criteria for CD: ① Diagnostic criteria for UCD: single-site lymphadenopathy, histopathologically characteristic proliferation, exclusion of possible other primary diseases, generally no systemic symptoms or anemia, elevated immunoglobulins, etc. (except for plasma cell type), and long-term survival after tumor resection; ② Diagnostic criteria for MCD: characteristic histopathological changes, significant lymphadenopathy affecting multiple peripheral lymph nodes, multi-system involvement, and exclusion of other causes. In differential diagnosis, it should be distinguished from malignant lymphoma, lymph node tuberculosis, and nonspecific lymphadenitis. The differentiation from malignant lymphoma includes the following points: in malignant lymphoma, the lymph node structure is almost completely destroyed, with few vascular proliferations, and there is proliferation of monoclonal lymphocytes or Reed-Sternberg cells. For common diseases presenting with lymphadenopathy, such as nonspecific lymphadenitis and lymph node tuberculosis, the key points of differentiation are: ① on physical examination, the former often has tenderness upon palpation, while the latter has no obvious tenderness; ② blood biochemical tests may show elevated neutrophil levels in the former; ③ PPD test differentiation. 3.4 Treatment and Prognosis: Based on previous studies, CD should be treated surgically by excising the abnormal lymph nodes. For UCD, excision of the affected lymph nodes is the most effective method. Clinical follow-up by foreign scholars indicates that UCD has little tendency to recur after surgical treatment. It is worth mentioning that patients with systemic symptoms often have symptom resolution after excision of the affected lymph nodes. However, there are also individual reports indicating that UCD recurred 9 years after surgical treatment. A recent meta-analysis based on 278 case reports found that the mortality rate for patients undergoing surgical treatment for UCD is 3.8%, while for other treatment methods it is 17.6%. Therefore, although surgical methods are very effective, there is also a risk of failure and recurrence. Lesions that cannot be completely excised will progress, and more of these cases will either remain stable or show no obvious symptoms. Rituximab is a drug used to treat MCD and has also been attempted in patients with UCD that cannot be cleared, achieving good results. As for MCD, due to limitations in examination methods, surgical excision is not the primary treatment method. Immunotherapy and chemotherapy are the basic treatment strategies for MCD. Prognostic assessment for MCD is generally more conservative than for UCD. Currently, there are no complete randomized trial studies on MCD treatment methods, and the choice of treatment is limited by existing case reports. Rituximab used alone or in combination with cell inhibitors has shown effectiveness in many patients. Clinical responses to rituximab include a decrease in serum IL-6 levels and a decrease in cytokines in other HIV-positive patient cases as HHV-8 virus levels decline. In more severe disease cases, when signs of organ failure and decreased physical strength appear, rituximab combined with chemotherapy drugs such as etoposide can increase the 5-year survival rate to 90%. However, chemotherapy drugs must be used in combination regimens: monoclonal antibody therapy combined with vincristine or etoposide can suppress MCD, but once discontinued, symptoms will recur within weeks. The latest chemotherapy regimens, such as CHOP (cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone) or CVAD (cyclophosphamide, pirarubicin, vincristine, and dexamethasone), have shown durable efficacy in over 50% of patients. 3.5 Related Disease Course: Although CD is primarily a disease characterized by lymph node lesions, both UCD and MCD may be associated with the occurrence of other diseases. In a series of cases in France, 8 UCD patients were found to have concurrent malignant lymphoma, of which 6 were B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma and 2 were Hodgkin lymphoma. Among the 8 patients, 3 had both UCD and NHL, even though the average diagnostic interval between the former and the latter was 46 months, and the pathophysiological relationship between the two remains unclear. Additionally, nearly 15% of UCD patients develop paraneoplastic pemphigus. MCD patients have similar associated diseases and a higher probability of multi-organ dysfunction compared to UCD patients. For example, MCD patients have a higher risk of malignant tumors, such as Kaposi's sarcoma and hematologic malignancies. 13% of MCD patients also have Kaposi's sarcoma. NHL has a significant relationship with MCD: 15% to 20% of patients have concurrent or developed NHL, with a poor prognosis and a mortality rate as high as 85%. HHV and HIV co-infection is considered an important risk factor for the development of NHL from MCD. Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma is the most common lymphoma in HHV-positive MCD patients, which is also a unique classification in the 2008 WHO classification of malignant tumors of lymphoid origin. Although the underlying mechanisms have yet to be determined, Hodgkin's disease may be related to MCD. MCD is also associated with POEMS syndrome, which includes polyneuropathy (P), organomegaly (O), endocrinopathy (E), monoclonal gammopathy (M, also known as M-protein), and skin changes (S).

[Case Introduction]  
Chief Complaint:  
[Discussion] 3.1 Overview of the Disease and Causes: Currently, there is no literature reporting the incidence of Castleman Disease (CD). Reports indicate that the average age of patients abroad is 35.6 years, while in China it is 33.1 years. The incidence is more common in females, with a male-to-female ratio of 1:4, although some literature reports no gender difference in incidence. Clinically, CD can be divided into localized (Unicentric CD, UCD) and multicentric (Multicentric CD, MCD). UCD is more common in youth, with a median age of about 20 years, where 90% of localized cases are of the hyaline vascular type and 10% are of the plasma cell type. MCD is less common than UCD, with a median age of 57 years and a male-to-female incidence ratio of 1:2. The disease can occur in any area where lymph nodes are present, with reports indicating that the mediastinum is the most common site in China (60%-70%), followed by the neck (10%-14%), abdomen (5%-10%), and axilla (2%-4%). International reports indicate that the most common sites for localized cases are the mediastinum (30%), neck (23%), abdomen (20%), retroperitoneal space (17%), axilla (5%), groin (3%), and pelvis (2%). Previous studies on the etiology of CD suggest that infection with the gamma herpesvirus HHV-8, elevated serum interleukin-6 levels, VEGF-guided angiogenesis, and atypical proliferation of follicular dendritic cells are correlated with the occurrence and development of CD. 3.2 Clinical Manifestations: UCD patients often have no subjective symptoms and are incidentally found to have painless masses during imaging examinations. A small number of patients may experience cough, dyspnea, or other systemic symptoms. Most UCD patients present with non-progressive clinical manifestations. In contrast, MCD is associated with systemic manifestations of inflammation: fever, night sweats, weight loss, and fatigue. MCD patients often have peripheral lymphadenopathy affecting multiple lymph nodes in the same region, with abnormal laboratory test results such as anemia, hypoalbuminemia, hypergammaglobulinemia, and elevated ESR, IL-6, and IL-10 levels. When these clinical manifestations and laboratory abnormalities occur simultaneously, they often suggest the possibility of MCD. Imaging examinations typically show space-occupying lesions or lymphadenopathy without obvious characteristics. Castleman disease occurring in mediastinal lymph nodes often appears as high-density mass shadows on chest X-rays, with well-defined or lobulated margins, which can be solitary or multiple, commonly showing indirect signs of widened mediastinum, affecting lung parenchyma. Ultrasound examinations typically show homogeneous hypoechoic masses with clear and intact capsules; larger lesions (greater than 5 cm) may appear as heterogeneous hypoechoic masses with uneven capsules. CT examinations typically show soft tissue mass shadows, with dynamic scanning changes resembling arteries. MRI examinations typically show low signal on T1-weighted images and high signal on T2-weighted images. MCD may also show splenomegaly and abdominal or pleural effusion on imaging examinations. This case presents as a space-occupying lesion in the right neck region II, with relatively consistent internal structure, clear boundaries, no obvious adhesion to surrounding tissues, and imaging characteristics of a benign tumor without specific changes. 3.3 Diagnosis and Differential Diagnosis: Due to the different sites of disease occurrence, diverse clinical manifestations, and non-specific clinical symptoms, preoperative diagnosis is challenging. Preoperative CT examinations are somewhat helpful in establishing the diagnosis, showing lesions as homogeneous density, with smooth or irregular soft tissue mass margins. CT enhancement scans show that the enhancement degree of hyaline vascular type UCD lesions is slightly lower than or synchronously significantly enhanced with adjacent large blood vessels; the significant enhancement of the mass is due to the presence of numerous blood vessels and rich capillaries, while plasma cell UCD shows uneven mild to moderate enhancement due to fewer vascular components, lacking characteristic manifestations. MCD typically shows no obvious mass on CT, often presenting one or more lymph nodes of similar size, with mild to moderate enhancement. In the latter two cases, the imaging findings are atypical, making preoperative diagnosis difficult. Early definitive diagnosis of this disease ultimately relies on histopathological examination. For CD, regardless of clinical or pathological classification, there are common histological features: ① intact basic structure of lymph nodes; ② lymphoid follicles and vascular proliferation, with glassy degeneration of vessels in hyaline vascular type follicles and atrophy of germinal centers; plasma cell type shows numerous plasma cells in the follicular stroma and hyperplasia of germinal centers. Frizzera proposed diagnostic criteria for CD: ① Diagnostic criteria for UCD: single-site lymphadenopathy, histopathologically characteristic proliferation, exclusion of possible other primary diseases, generally no systemic symptoms or anemia, elevated immunoglobulins, etc. (except for plasma cell type), and long-term survival after tumor resection; ② Diagnostic criteria for MCD: characteristic histopathological changes, significant lymphadenopathy affecting multiple peripheral lymph nodes, multi-system involvement, and exclusion of other causes. In differential diagnosis, it should be distinguished from malignant lymphoma, lymph node tuberculosis, and nonspecific lymphadenitis. The differentiation from malignant lymphoma includes the following points: in malignant lymphoma, the lymph node structure is almost completely destroyed, with few vascular proliferations, and there is proliferation of monoclonal lymphocytes or Reed-Sternberg cells. For common diseases presenting with lymphadenopathy, such as nonspecific lymphadenitis and lymph node tuberculosis, the key points of differentiation are: ① on physical examination, the former often has tenderness upon palpation, while the latter has no obvious tenderness; ② blood biochemical tests may show elevated neutrophil levels in the former; ③ PPD test differentiation. 3.4 Treatment and Prognosis: Based on previous studies, CD should be treated surgically by excising the abnormal lymph nodes. For UCD, excision of the affected lymph nodes is the most effective method. Clinical follow-up by foreign scholars indicates that UCD has little tendency to recur after surgical treatment. It is worth mentioning that patients with systemic symptoms often have symptom resolution after excision of the affected lymph nodes. However, there are also individual reports indicating that UCD recurred 9 years after surgical treatment. A recent meta-analysis based on 278 case reports found that the mortality rate for patients undergoing surgical treatment for UCD is 3.8%, while for other treatment methods it is 17.6%. Therefore, although surgical methods are very effective, there is also a risk of failure and recurrence. Lesions that cannot be completely excised will progress, and more of these cases will either remain stable or show no obvious symptoms. Rituximab is a drug used to treat MCD and has also been attempted in patients with UCD that cannot be cleared, achieving good results. As for MCD, due to limitations in examination methods, surgical excision is not the primary treatment method. Immunotherapy and chemotherapy are the basic treatment strategies for MCD. Prognostic assessment for MCD is generally more conservative than for UCD. Currently, there are no complete randomized trial studies on MCD treatment methods, and the choice of treatment is limited by existing case reports. Rituximab used alone or in combination with cell inhibitors has shown effectiveness in many patients. Clinical responses to rituximab include a decrease in serum IL-6 levels and a decrease in cytokines in other HIV-positive patient cases as HHV-8 virus levels decline. In more severe disease cases, when signs of organ failure and decreased physical strength appear, rituximab combined with chemotherapy drugs such as etoposide can increase the 5-year survival rate to 90%. However, chemotherapy drugs must be used in combination regimens: monoclonal antibody therapy combined with vincristine or etoposide can suppress MCD, but once discontinued, symptoms will recur within weeks. The latest chemotherapy regimens, such as CHOP (cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone) or CVAD (cyclophosphamide, pirarubicin, vincristine, and dexamethasone), have shown durable efficacy in over 50% of patients. 3.5 Related Disease Course: Although CD is primarily a disease characterized by lymph node lesions, both UCD and MCD may be associated with the occurrence of other diseases. In a series of cases in France, 8 UCD patients were found to have concurrent malignant lymphoma, of which 6 were B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma and 2 were Hodgkin lymphoma. Among the 8 patients, 3 had both UCD and NHL, even though the average diagnostic interval between the former and the latter was 46 months, and the pathophysiological relationship between the two remains unclear. Additionally, nearly 15% of UCD patients develop paraneoplastic pemphigus. MCD patients have similar associated diseases and a higher probability of multi-organ dysfunction compared to UCD patients. For example, MCD patients have a higher risk of malignant tumors, such as Kaposi's sarcoma and hematologic malignancies. 13% of MCD patients also have Kaposi's sarcoma. NHL has a significant relationship with MCD: 15% to 20% of patients have concurrent or developed NHL, with a poor prognosis and a mortality rate as high as 85%. HHV and HIV co-infection is considered an important risk factor for the development of NHL from MCD. Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma is the most common lymphoma in HHV-positive MCD patients, which is also a unique classification in the 2008 WHO classification of malignant tumors of lymphoid origin. Although the underlying mechanisms have yet to be determined, Hodgkin's disease may be related to MCD. MCD is also associated with POEMS syndrome, which includes polyneuropathy (P), organomegaly (O), endocrinopathy (E), monoclonal gammopathy (M, also known as M-protein), and skin changes (S).

Chief Complaint:  
Gradual onset of poor mood and sleep for 5 months, worsening for 1 week  
Present Illness History:  
The patient experienced a flood in her hometown on June 10, which washed away her house and rice. She gradually developed a poor mood, excessive worry, and concerns about her children's education costs and food, often sighing. She had poor sleep at night, sometimes waking up at 1 AM and finding it difficult to fall back asleep, getting up at 5 AM to work. One week ago, the patient repeatedly expressed that she had no money, that no matter how much she earned, it was not enough for her children's tuition, often calculating how much she needed to earn. At times, she mentioned calling her children back to harm each other, stating that even if ten family members died, it would not be enough. Sometimes she would tell others that she wanted to help save her eldest child and offer him as a son to someone else. She only slept two to three hours a day and felt fatigued during the day. Two days ago, she began to repeatedly shake her head, and yesterday afternoon, she suddenly became stiff in her limbs, closed her eyes, and did not respond when called. Her family wanted to take her to the hospital, but the patient refused treatment, claiming she was not ill and that spending so much money would be better used for her children's education. The family took her to Guangdong Sanjiu Brain Hospital, where she was diagnosed with "depression" and treated with "valproate extended-release tablets + aripiprazole orally disintegrating tablets + paroxetine tablets," but the patient refused to take the medication. Today, the family requested hospitalization. Emergency assessment indicated that the patient had **delusions**, and she was admitted to our department under "involuntary circumstances." Since the onset of her illness, her diet and sleep have been acceptable, her bowel and bladder functions are normal, and there has been no significant change in weight.  
Past Medical History:  
Denies history of physical illness, head trauma, surgery, coma/seizures, sexually transmitted diseases, infectious diseases, food or drug allergies, blood transfusions, and has a standard vaccination history. Personal history: 7 siblings, 5 alive. Pregnancy and birth: Mother was healthy during pregnancy; term delivery; vaginal birth; no asphyxia or hypoxia at birth, Apgar score unknown. Childhood development: Similar to peers, spoke at over 1 year, walked at over 1 year, controlled bowel and bladder at over 3 years. Family and social environment: No special issues. Education: Good preschool education; entered school at age 7; primary school performance: good; middle school performance: good; high school performance: good. Interpersonal relationships: Many friends, good relationships with friends. Personality traits and coping style before illness: Cheerful, extroverted. Stressful event: Flood destroyed her house. Family history: Denies any neurological or psychiatric disorders in three generations of parents.  
Physical Examination:  
T: 36.7°C, P: 90 beats/min, R: 19 breaths/min, BP: 140/86 mmHg  
Lung sounds clear bilaterally, no significant dry or wet rales heard. Heart rate 90 beats/min, regular rhythm, no significant murmurs in valve areas. Abdomen soft, no tenderness or rebound tenderness, bowel sounds 4 times/min. Mental examination: Clear consciousness, age-appropriate appearance, passive cooperation in contact, relevant responses, accurate orientation. No perceptual disturbances noted. Thought process coherent, sensitive and suspicious, repeatedly discussing money issues, claiming recent nonsensical talk and actions were due to someone doing something to her, and that some believers thought she might be possessed by a ghost; emotional state shows anxiety, with feelings of guilt, stating she has done wrong and has no money to support her children. Intelligence and memory roughly assessed as normal. No insight, believes she has no issues and does not need hospitalization.  
Auxiliary Examinations:  
2019-11-12 Guangdong Sanjiu Brain Hospital cranial MRI showed "1. Slight degenerative lesions in bilateral frontal lobes; 2. Some vacuoles in the sella turcica; 3. Inflammation in bilateral maxillary sinuses, ethmoid sinuses, and frontal sinuses." Chest X-ray indicated "aorta protruding to the left." The patient feels unwell.  

Patient's attention is normal.  

Patient has not contemplated death.
